nativeRange.habitat: Slow to moderately flowing rivers, streams, and flooded areas with dense riparian vegetation; clear to slightly turbid water over sandy or muddy substrates

decorPreferences: planted areas with fine-leaved plants (hornwort, water sprite, java moss) for cover
open central swimming space for schooling
dim or filtered lighting with floating plants
dark substrate to enhance colour contrast
driftwood and leaf litter for natural biotope feel

diet.notes: Readily accepts high-quality micro-pellets, small flake food, and freeze-dried or frozen foods including brine shrimp, daphnia, and bloodworms. Wild fish feed on small invertebrates, insects, worms, and plant material. Variety improves coloration and condition.

goodWith: other small peaceful tetras (rummy nose, ember tetra, neon tetra)
avoidWith: long-finned fish (angelfish, bettas, fancy guppies) — fin-nipping risk in groups under 8
aggressive or boisterous species that outcompete at feeding
very small nano fish or shrimp fry that could be eaten

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by VGW2006 (800x245). Planted-aquarium group shot of a small school showing the diagnostic silvery-blue bodies and bright blood-red fins; best represents this schooling species as kept, though modest resolution.
Photo by Zakqary Roy (2048x1536). Tack-sharp in-hand lateral view of a live wild-caught individual; vivid red caudal, anal and dorsal fins against a silvery-green body confirm ID.
Photo by Zakqary Roy (1820x1359). Sharp in-hand shot, whole fish in frame, red-tipped fins clearly visible; alternate angle of a live vibrant specimen.

summary: The Bloodfin Tetra is a schooling characin from the Paraná River basin, commonly regarded as one of the hardier small tetras in the hobby, known for its striking red-tipped fins contrasting with a silvery-blue body. Unusually cool-water tolerant (18–28°C), it thrives in unheated tanks and is exceptionally long-lived for a tetra at 5–7 years.
careNotes: The Bloodfin Tetra (Aphyocharax anisitsi) is native to the Paraná River drainage of Argentina and Paraguay, where water temperatures can drop to 18°C in winter — making it one of the few tetras genuinely suited to unheated or minimally heated aquariums in temperate climates. A minimum tank of 60 L with at least 60 cm length is recommended for a school of 8 or more; they are active midwater swimmers that need horizontal swimming space. Water chemistry is forgiving across a wide range (pH 6.0–7.8, gH 2–20), reflecting their adaptability to varying conditions in the wild Paraná floodplain. Maintaining groups of at least 8 helps suppress a mild fin-nipping tendency; smaller groups (under 6) can result in harassment of slower, long-finned tankmates. The species is sometimes confused with the True Bloodfin (Aphyocharax rathbuni), which displays more extensive red colouring covering much of the body rather than being restricted to the fins. A. anisitsi is generally considered the hardier of the two and tends to be more readily available in the aquarium trade. The species is broadly regarded as a good choice for beginners, thanks to its tolerance of a wide temperature range and a lifespan of 5–7 years — unusually long for a small characid. Providing a varied diet of quality flake, micro-pellets, and occasional frozen foods helps maintain vibrant fin coloration.
breedingNotes: Bloodfin Tetras are egg scatterers that will spawn among fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop. A well-conditioned pair or small group can be triggered by slightly cooler, soft, acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 2–5, temperature 22–24°C) combined with live or frozen foods. Adults readily eat their own eggs, so parents should be removed or a breeding net used after spawning. Eggs hatch in 24–36 hours; fry are free-swimming within 4–5 days and accept infusoria, egg yolk, and commercial fry foods. Breeding in a dedicated setup is generally considered moderately achievable.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Aphyocharax anisitsi
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Paraná River basin, Argentina/Paraguay), water parameters (temperature 18–28°C, pH 6.0–7.8, hardness 36–357 ppm), adult size (5 cm), temperament, schooling requirements, diet, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Aphyocharax anisitsi
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Characidae, Characiformes), adult size (to 5.5 cm TL), native range (Paraná drainage, Argentina/Paraguay/Brazil), temperature range, and lifespan data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Bloodfin Tetra Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade care guide confirming beginner suitability, minimum school size of 6–8, unheated tank compatibility, and recommended tank size of 15+ gallons (57 L) for a school.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Aphyocharax anisitsi species profile
additional.notes: Confirmed cool-water tolerance and distinction from A. rathbuni (True Bloodfin); noted fin-nipping tendency in small groups and long captive lifespan of 5–7 years.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Bloodfin Tetras in the Aquarium
additional.notes: Historical trade context; confirmed wide availability, hardiness, and notes on distinguishing A. anisitsi from A. rathbuni by extent of red colouration.

fish.breedingNotes: Bloodfin Tetras are egg scatterers that spawn among fine-leaved plants or a spawning mop in soft, slightly acidic water. Adults will eat their own eggs, so parents should be removed promptly after spawning. Fry hatch in 24–36 hours and initially accept infusoria or finely powdered fry food.
